question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Short answer — start as 1st‑level (helpdesk) triage, escalate to 2nd‑level only if one of the escalation triggers below is met. Rationale - When Device Manager shows the camera but Teams (desktop) and Teams in the browser cannot use it, the pattern most commonly points to an OS‑level permission/configuration issue (Windows privacy settings or app permissions) or a driver/service regression triggered by an update. In the documented case the camera was visible in Device Manager but app access was blocked by Windows privacy settings and the helpdesk restored camera access by enabling the OS/app permissions; that ticket was handled and closed at first line

Root cause was missing operating system permission and blocked device setting. Steps performed according to Blueprint. Kind regards 07.10.25 09:12 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 07.10.25 09:30 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please confirm whether the user is using Windows or Mac and whether the camera is available in the browser (e.g., Teams Web)? Thank you. 07.10.25 09:48 – Customer response Windows 11. The camera also does not work in the browser. 07.10.25 10:25 – Internal note Connected to the user via remote session. The camera is visible in Device Manager, but Teams has no access. In Windows privacy settings, camera access for apps is disabled. Additionally, Teams is not enabled in the list of allowed apps. After adjusting the camera and app permissions according to the Blueprint, the camera is available again in Teams. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: : Quick tests (try in this order) 1. Open Windows Camera app — does it show a live image? (If yes, hardware/driver are likely OK; focus on Teams/browser permissions.) 2. Settings → Privacy & security → Camera — ensure “Camera access for this device”, “Let apps access your camera” and “Let desktop apps access your camera” are ON and Teams/browser are allowed. After changing, restart Teams and re‑test. (This exact fix resolved the documented ticket)

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . 3. Test Teams in an Incognito/private browser window (to exclude cached tokens/extensions) and check browser site camera permissions. 4. In Teams desktop: sign out, clear cache (or Repair app), restart, and test again. 5. Device Manager: try Disable → Enable the camera; inspect Properties → Driver for Provider / Driver Version / Driver Date. Capture those values if present. If Device Manager shows a warning or error code, collect it (that pushes toward escalation). 6. Temporarily close virtual‑camera apps and check AV/endpoint webcam‑protection settings (do not leave AV disabled longer than necessary). If disabling webcam protection restores access, capture AV logs/settings. Useful commands / quick collections to paste into the ticket - Get driver metadata (PowerShell): Get-CimInstance Win32_PnPSignedDriver | Where-Object { $_.DeviceName -match "(?i)camera" } | Select DeviceName, Manufacturer, DriverVersion, DriverDate - Get most recent installed update (PowerShell): Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending | Select-Object -First 3 - Collect Teams logs (user): press Ctrl+Alt+Shift+1 while reproducing the issue (creates a logs ZIP in Downloads). (These collection steps are part of the recommended evidence set used in the runbook)

Escalation (when to treat as 2nd‑level) Escalate to 2nd/endpoint engineering when any of the following apply — these are the documented escalation triggers in the runbooks and incidents: - The Windows privacy setting is greyed out, reverts after change, or gpresult/rsop shows a policy disabling camera access (i.e., GPO/Intune enforcement). - Device Manager reports driver errors (Code 10, driver failing to start) or driver reinstall/rollback is required and needs admin/vendor driver packages. - Evidence the recent Windows update or a pushed driver caused a regression and requires rollback or vendor escalation. - Multiple users or many devices in the same OU/MDM group show identical symptoms (suggests a deployment/policy issue). - Event Viewer shows CameraFrameServer/driver/kernel errors that need deeper debugging. When escalating, include the artifacts below — they speed the 2nd‑level investigation

[Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)] REPORT 131: # Microsoft Teams (Direct Routing) PSTN Disruption & Device Permission Issues This community centers on Microsoft Teams as the enterprise collaboration platform where external PSTN calls via Direct Routing experienced functional failures (one‑way audio, ~30s disconnects, and signaling anomalies) while internal Teams‑to‑Teams functions remained usable. Investigation used Teams Admin Center and Direct Routing/SBC logs and referenced an internal Federation & External Access blueprint to guide remediation steps. Endpoint troubleshooting showed the call failures persisted across different user devices and network attachments, indicating a network/session or signaling issue rather than purely local client faults; separately, camera/video availability was caused by OS app‑permission/privacy settings that were corrected for affected users. Microsoft 365 service health was reported normal during the incident, narrowing the scope to Direct Routing/session behavior and local OS permissions for camera access. [Data: Entities (122,327,331,739,672,497,454); Relationships (522,523,273,1329,829,1332,1328,720)] ## Primary symptoms: external PSTN via Direct Routing experienced one‑way audio, early call termination (~30s), and impaired external presence/chat signaling while internal Teams features remained functional The incident primarily affected external PSTN traffic arriving via Direct Routing; customers observed one‑way audio where either the internal user could not hear the external PSTN caller or the external participant could not hear the user, and calls often disconnected after approximately 30 seconds. The Teams client could appear “Connected” even when audio was missing, and presence sharing for external partners showed as “Offline.” Chat delivery and call signaling to/from external parties were impacted, although internal Teams‑to‑Teams chat, video and calls continued to operate. These observations are recorded in the incident description of the Microsoft Teams entity and its associated troubleshooting notes [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (522,273)]. ## Evidence points to session/signaling or network path issues (Direct Routing/SBC) rather than endpoint hardware failure Device‑level troubleshooting (testing different user devices and replacing docking station with a direct LAN cable) did not resolve the PSTN call failures, indicating the fault persisted across endpoints and connectivity methods and therefore was likely session or network related rather than a specific client or docking hardware problem. Additionally, Direct Routing logs were analyzed and showed SIP signaling establishment with sessions terminating after a short interval (~30s), which supports a session timer or SBC behavior as a likely contributor. Microsoft 365 service status was reported normal during the troubleshooting window, further focusing the investigation on local/network Direct Routing components rather than a broad Microsoft outage. These points are documented in the End User Devices and Direct Routing Logs entities and their relationships to Microsoft Teams and Microsoft 365 checks [Data: Entities (331,327,454); Relationships (523,522,720)]. ## Teams Admin Center diagnostic data corroborated that incoming PSTN calls were not reaching Teams as the final inbound target for some numbers Support used the Teams Admin Center to monitor inbound call events and diagnostics; the Admin Center showed no incoming call entries for some affected numbers, corroborating that calls were not being presented to Teams as the inbound routing target in those cases. This diagnostic observation was an important local validation step to confirm where in the call path the problem manifested (i.e., calls failing before or at the Teams ingress). The use of the Teams Admin Center as a validation source is recorded in the relationships tied to Microsoft Teams diagnostics [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (273)]. ## Camera/video problems were caused by OS privacy/app permission settings, not device drivers Although the camera hardware is present and visible in Device Manager, Windows camera privacy and per‑app permissions had been disabled for apps; this blocked Teams and browsers from accessing the camera. When OS permissions were adjusted (following the internal blueprint guidance), camera functionality in both the Teams desktop client and the browser was restored. These observations distinguish the PSTN/audio issue (network/session related) from the camera/video issue (OS permissions), and the investigation documented both the camera entity state and the OS permissions adjustments. This is supported by the Camera and Operating System Permissions entities and their relationships showing permissions changes restored camera access [Data: Entities (672,739,738); Relationships (1332,1329,1328)]. ## Internal Federation & External Access blueprint was an active reference and guided remediation/configuration changes An internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS FEDERATION & EXTERNAL ACCESS – VERSION 1.4) that documents differences between federation and presence sharing, the impact of Privacy Mode, and Direct Routing considerations was referenced and used to guide remediation. Configuration adjustments consistent with the blueprint were applied during the investigation and remediation. The blueprint provided procedural and configuration guidance relevant to presence, external access, and permissions that influenced the remediation steps taken by responders. This is explicitly documented in the Teams‑to‑Blueprint relationship and the incident notes. [Data: Entities (497,122); Relationships (829)]. ## Operational impact: external voice communications degraded while Teams remained usable as a coordination channel during response Although internal Teams communications (chat/video/calls) continued to function, the disruption of external PSTN traffic via Direct Routing created a material operational impact for interactions with external callers. The Teams client was nevertheless used as an operational communications channel during the incident response, including on the date recorded, which indicates that responders relied on the platform for coordination while remediating PSTN and permission issues. This split impact (internal usable, external degraded) and the operational use of Teams during remediation are described in the Microsoft Teams entity notes and relationships. [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (829,273)]. ## Key diagnostics available but further targeted SBC/Direct Routing session analysis and monitoring are required Direct Routing logs and Teams Admin Center data were available and reviewed; logs indicated sessions were being terminated after a short interval despite signaling initially establishing. While these diagnostics narrowed the scope to session behavior, additional targeted analysis on SBC session timers, SIP session timers (e.g., INVITE/200 OK/ACK and session timer handling, re‑INVITE behavior), NAT/timeouts, and any intermediary media proxies is necessary to establish a definitive root cause and prevent recurrence. The presence of these logs and the Admin Center entries provides a foundation for more prescriptive remediation work. These diagnostic sources are recorded as Direct Routing Logs and Teams Admin Center relationships to Microsoft Teams in the dataset. [Data: Entities (327,122); Relationships (522,273)]. ## Remediation and governance gaps to address (monitoring runbooks, escalation paths, and blueprint operationalization) The incident shows three areas for operational improvement: (1) formalize a runbook for Direct Routing/PSTN session failures that includes SBC session timer checks, log collection points, and quick validation steps in Teams Admin Center and SBC; (2) codify OS privacy/permission checks into onboarding and support triage procedures so camera/video permission issues are resolved faster; and (3) operationalize the Federation & External Access blueprint into a change‑control checklist and escalation pathway for any configuration changes affecting external access. These gaps are implied by the remediation steps that referenced the blueprint, the device/permissions fixes performed by an employee, and the diagnostic artifacts used during troubleshooting, which are all present in the dataset. Implementing these items will reduce time‑to‑resolution and recurrence risk. [Data: Entities (497,739,327); Relationships (829,1328,522)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

[Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)] REPORT 131: # Microsoft Teams (Direct Routing) PSTN Disruption & Device Permission Issues This community centers on Microsoft Teams as the enterprise collaboration platform where external PSTN calls via Direct Routing experienced functional failures (one‑way audio, ~30s disconnects, and signaling anomalies) while internal Teams‑to‑Teams functions remained usable. Investigation used Teams Admin Center and Direct Routing/SBC logs and referenced an internal Federation & External Access blueprint to guide remediation steps. Endpoint troubleshooting showed the call failures persisted across different user devices and network attachments, indicating a network/session or signaling issue rather than purely local client faults; separately, camera/video availability was caused by OS app‑permission/privacy settings that were corrected for affected users. Microsoft 365 service health was reported normal during the incident, narrowing the scope to Direct Routing/session behavior and local OS permissions for camera access. [Data: Entities (122,327,331,739,672,497,454); Relationships (522,523,273,1329,829,1332,1328,720)] ## Primary symptoms: external PSTN via Direct Routing experienced one‑way audio, early call termination (~30s), and impaired external presence/chat signaling while internal Teams features remained functional The incident primarily affected external PSTN traffic arriving via Direct Routing; customers observed one‑way audio where either the internal user could not hear the external PSTN caller or the external participant could not hear the user, and calls often disconnected after approximately 30 seconds. The Teams client could appear “Connected” even when audio was missing, and presence sharing for external partners showed as “Offline.” Chat delivery and call signaling to/from external parties were impacted, although internal Teams‑to‑Teams chat, video and calls continued to operate. These observations are recorded in the incident description of the Microsoft Teams entity and its associated troubleshooting notes [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (522,273)]. ## Evidence points to session/signaling or network path issues (Direct Routing/SBC) rather than endpoint hardware failure Device‑level troubleshooting (testing different user devices and replacing docking station with a direct LAN cable) did not resolve the PSTN call failures, indicating the fault persisted across endpoints and connectivity methods and therefore was likely session or network related rather than a specific client or docking hardware problem. Additionally, Direct Routing logs were analyzed and showed SIP signaling establishment with sessions terminating after a short interval (~30s), which supports a session timer or SBC behavior as a likely contributor. Microsoft 365 service status was reported normal during the troubleshooting window, further focusing the investigation on local/network Direct Routing components rather than a broad Microsoft outage. These points are documented in the End User Devices and Direct Routing Logs entities and their relationships to Microsoft Teams and Microsoft 365 checks [Data: Entities (331,327,454); Relationships (523,522,720)]. ## Teams Admin Center diagnostic data corroborated that incoming PSTN calls were not reaching Teams as the final inbound target for some numbers Support used the Teams Admin Center to monitor inbound call events and diagnostics; the Admin Center showed no incoming call entries for some affected numbers, corroborating that calls were not being presented to Teams as the inbound routing target in those cases. This diagnostic observation was an important local validation step to confirm where in the call path the problem manifested (i.e., calls failing before or at the Teams ingress). The use of the Teams Admin Center as a validation source is recorded in the relationships tied to Microsoft Teams diagnostics [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (273)]. ## Camera/video problems were caused by OS privacy/app permission settings, not device drivers Although the camera hardware is present and visible in Device Manager, Windows camera privacy and per‑app permissions had been disabled for apps; this blocked Teams and browsers from accessing the camera. When OS permissions were adjusted (following the internal blueprint guidance), camera functionality in both the Teams desktop client and the browser was restored. These observations distinguish the PSTN/audio issue (network/session related) from the camera/video issue (OS permissions), and the investigation documented both the camera entity state and the OS permissions adjustments. This is supported by the Camera and Operating System Permissions entities and their relationships showing permissions changes restored camera access [Data: Entities (672,739,738); Relationships (1332,1329,1328)]. ## Internal Federation & External Access blueprint was an active reference and guided remediation/configuration changes An internal blueprint (BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS FEDERATION & EXTERNAL ACCESS – VERSION 1.4) that documents differences between federation and presence sharing, the impact of Privacy Mode, and Direct Routing considerations was referenced and used to guide remediation. Configuration adjustments consistent with the blueprint were applied during the investigation and remediation. The blueprint provided procedural and configuration guidance relevant to presence, external access, and permissions that influenced the remediation steps taken by responders. This is explicitly documented in the Teams‑to‑Blueprint relationship and the incident notes. [Data: Entities (497,122); Relationships (829)]. ## Operational impact: external voice communications degraded while Teams remained usable as a coordination channel during response Although internal Teams communications (chat/video/calls) continued to function, the disruption of external PSTN traffic via Direct Routing created a material operational impact for interactions with external callers. The Teams client was nevertheless used as an operational communications channel during the incident response, including on the date recorded, which indicates that responders relied on the platform for coordination while remediating PSTN and permission issues. This split impact (internal usable, external degraded) and the operational use of Teams during remediation are described in the Microsoft Teams entity notes and relationships. [Data: Entities (122); Relationships (829,273)]. ## Key diagnostics available but further targeted SBC/Direct Routing session analysis and monitoring are required Direct Routing logs and Teams Admin Center data were available and reviewed; logs indicated sessions were being terminated after a short interval despite signaling initially establishing. While these diagnostics narrowed the scope to session behavior, additional targeted analysis on SBC session timers, SIP session timers (e.g., INVITE/200 OK/ACK and session timer handling, re‑INVITE behavior), NAT/timeouts, and any intermediary media proxies is necessary to establish a definitive root cause and prevent recurrence. The presence of these logs and the Admin Center entries provides a foundation for more prescriptive remediation work. These diagnostic sources are recorded as Direct Routing Logs and Teams Admin Center relationships to Microsoft Teams in the dataset. [Data: Entities (327,122); Relationships (522,273)]. ## Remediation and governance gaps to address (monitoring runbooks, escalation paths, and blueprint operationalization) The incident shows three areas for operational improvement: (1) formalize a runbook for Direct Routing/PSTN session failures that includes SBC session timer checks, log collection points, and quick validation steps in Teams Admin Center and SBC; (2) codify OS privacy/permission checks into onboarding and support triage procedures so camera/video permission issues are resolved faster; and (3) operationalize the Federation & External Access blueprint into a change‑control checklist and escalation pathway for any configuration changes affecting external access. These gaps are implied by the remediation steps that referenced the blueprint, the device/permissions fixes performed by an employee, and the diagnostic artifacts used during troubleshooting, which are all present in the dataset. Implementing these items will reduce time‑to‑resolution and recurrence risk. [Data: Entities (497,739,327); Relationships (829,1328,522)].
